coredata.dc:title: A regenerable and reducing false-positive fluorescent switch for detection of β-amyloid 1−42 oligomers
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Shuang
Li, Shunling
Tang, Congkui
Bai, Xiongfei
Ran, Xin
Qu, Qing
Li, Lei
Yang, Long
coredata.dc:description: The conventional fluorescence analysis methods for disease identification are vulnerable to the restriction with false-positive. Here, a fluorescent switch with high efficiency and regeneration by the black phosphorus (BP) nanosheets-regulated was developed to overcome false-positive issue in the assay of β-amyloid1−42 oligomers (Aβ) process. The Aβ was rapidly recognized using the fluorescent emitter‐nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CDs) under the regulation of BP nanosheets, while the N-CDs alone cannot recognize Aβ without the introduction of BP. The fluorescence analysis methods exhibited a wide sensing range of 0.25–15.0 ng/mL and a low detection limit of 83 pg/mL for Aβ analysis, which was superior to the reported fluorescence analysis method. Further, BP nanosheets were recycled, demonstrating the fluorescent switch with highly efficient, stable, and regenerable. This provides a new idea for developing high-efficiency and high-precision fluorescence detection platform.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BP nanosheets
Regenerable
Reducing false-positive
Fluorescent switch

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of Aβ by fluorescent switch
Evaluating the selectivity of fluorescence analysis method
Fluorescent switch reduce false positives and regeneration

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and (B) High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of bare BP nanosheets. Inset: corresponding HRTEM image; (C) AFM image of ultrathin BP nanosheets; (D) Raman spectra of the crystal BP (blue) and BP nanosheets (red); (E) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra of BP nanosheets; (F) High-resolution P 2p XPS spectra of BP nanosheets. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(A) HR-TEM image of N-CDs (Inset: typical N-CDs with lattice parameter of 0.21 nm); (B) XPS spectra of N-CDs; (C)–(D) XPS spectra of C 1s and O 1s, for N-CDs.

Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs@Anti Aβ from 300 nm to 400 nm excitation wavelengths for (A) and (B). Measurements of the N-CDs@Anti Aβ biosensor for the detection of Aβ.(C) Extinction spectra of the N-CDs@Anti Aβ in the presence of various BP nanosheets concentrations (0–0.25 mg/mL). (D) and their plots of the quenching efficiency (%). (E) Fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@Anti Aβ/BP nanosheets recovery with different Aβ concentrations from 0 to 20 ng/mL (F) The photographs of fluorescent responses to the diverse concentrations of Aβ.

(A) Normalized UV–Vis absorption spectra of BP nanosheets and fluorescence spectra for N-CDs@Anti-Aβ; (B) Zeta potential of N-CDs@Anti-Aβ and BP nanosheets; (C) fluorescence lifetime decay of N-CDs@Anti-Aβ without or with BP nanosheets (D).

(A) Fluorescence spectra and corresponding images of (1) N-CDs@Anti-Aβ, (2) N-CDs@Anti-Aβ/BP nanosheets, (3) N-CDs@Anti-Aβ/Aβ/BP nanosheets; (B) Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of BP nanosheets, Anti-Aβ and BP nanosheets@Anti-Aβ.
Schematic of the N-CDs@Anti-Aβ synthetic route and sensing strategy of BP nanosheets regulating N-CDs@Anti-Aβ for Aβ detection.

head.ce:title: A regenerable and reducing false-positive fluorescent switch for detection of β-amyloid 1−42 oligomers

College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Joint Research Center for Biomass Materials, Yunnan Province Key Lab of Wood Adhesives and Glued Products, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest Forestry University, Kunming 650224, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Southwest Forestry University, Kunming, 650224, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Southwest Forestry University, Kunming, 650224, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The conventional fluorescence analysis methods for disease identification are vulnerable to the restriction with false-positive. Here, a fluorescent switch with high efficiency and regeneration by the black phosphorus (BP) nanosheets-regulated was developed to overcome false-positive issue in the assay of β-amyloid1−42 oligomers (Aβ) process. The Aβ was rapidly recognized using the fluorescent emitter‐nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CDs) under the regulation of BP nanosheets, while the N-CDs alone cannot recognize Aβ without the introduction of BP. The fluorescence analysis methods exhibited a wide sensing range of 0.25–15.0 ng/mL and a low detection limit of 83 pg/mL for Aβ analysis, which was superior to the reported fluorescence analysis method. Further, BP nanosheets were recycled, demonstrating the fluorescent switch with highly efficient, stable, and regenerable. This provides a new idea for developing high-efficiency and high-precision fluorescence detection platform.

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer disease (AD), a nervous system disease, results memory loss and language disorders [1,2]. As a typical neurotoxic species, β-amyloid1−42 oligomers (Aβ) are one of the major contributors to Alzheimer disease [3]. Identifying Aβ protein will be beneficial to the early warning and diagnosis of Alzheimer disease. To date, many methods including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, fluorescence, electrochemical, and electrochemiluminescence have been used to identify Aβ protein [4–7]. Among them, the fluorescence analysis method integrated the advantages of antibody-antigen specific binding and fluorescent prob possessing overwhelming superiority owing to fast response, superb sensitivity, and user-friendly control [8]. However, the false-positive has been a bottleneck for fluorescence detection platform applications, due to hydrolysis of antibodies producing interfering substances leading to false-positive [9].

The carbon nanodots (CDs), 0D nano luminescent materials, were considered to be most ideal fluorescent biosensing materials, due to their photostability, facile functionalization, admirable biocompatibility, and water solubility [10]. Black phosphorus (BP) nanosheets had been reported to possess the ability of fluorescence quenched [11], thus potentially controlling the fluorescence of CDs ‘on’ and ‘off’ for the detection of substances. Whereas, most fluorescence analysis method is implemented in a single mode of “on” or “off”, which begets false-positive and less sensitive results [12]. More importantly, BP nanosheets were able to conjugate with antibodies and enhancing the stability of antibodies [13,14]. Besides, BP nanosheets had excellent surface activity, higher carrier mobility, and good biocompatibility [15]. Therefore, the introduction of BP nanosheets may improve the stability of Anti-Aβ to reduce fluorescence false-positive.

Here, a new and recyclable fluorescent switch was designed by combining N-CDs with BP nanosheets for sensitive detection of Aβ. Aminated CDs (N-CDs) specifically bound to Anti-Aβ were synthesized by hydrothermal method in ammonia using cellulose as carbon precursor (Scheme 1 ). Following, BP nanosheets act as a fluorescent switch for fluorescence detection platform to control the rapid recognition of Aβ by N-CDs@Anti-Aβ. The solution fluorescence remains “on” states in the N-CDs@Anti-Aβ, as well as the mixture N-CDs@Anti-Aβ and Aβ solution. The fluorescence was “off” states by the introduction of BP nanosheets in the mixture N-CDs@Anti-Aβ and Aβ solution, while the fluorescence is restored after removal of BP nanosheets, thus overcoming the false-positive for identifying Aβ by the fluorescence detection platform (Scheme 1). This offers new ideas to address the issue of false-positive in the detection of bio-detection and other targets by the fluorescent method.

Amyloid-β protein (Aβ), amyloid-β protein primary antibodies (Anti-Aβ) were purchased from GenScript Biotechnology co., ltd (Nanjing, China), Sigmacell Cellulose (Type 20, 20 μm) was purchased from Sigma (USA). DL-Lysine (Lys＞98% purity) was brought from Adamas Reagent Ltd (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA, 96–99%), ovalbumin (OVA), ammonium hydroxide solution, ferrous chloride (FeCl2), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were from Aladdin Ltd (Shanghai, China). Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 were obtained from the Tianjin Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). Methylglyoxal mouse monoclonal antibody (MGO), prostate-specific antigen (PSA), S-allyl-L-cysteine sulfoxide (ACSO) were brought from the Saitukang Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Zhengzhou, Chain). All other reagents in the experiments were of analytical grade and without additional purification.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were gained by a JEM 2100 TEM (JEOL, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were studied on the K-Alpha+ system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Raman spectra were recorded by a 400F PerkinElmer Raman spectrometer (Shelton, USA). Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a F-7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with slit width of 5 nm and a scan rate is 1200 nm/min. Zeta potential measurements were conducted by a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments, UK). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) were performed by a Nicolet Avatar 360 FT-IR (Nicolet, USA) with KBr pellets. UV–vis absorbance spectrum measurements were operated by a UV-2800A (Unico, China). Atomic force microscopy (AFM, Dimension Icon, BRUKER) was used to determine the surface topography and thickness of BP nanosheets.

The BP nanosheets were obtained by liquid-phase ultrasonic exfoliating through a top-down method. The preparation process of BP nanosheets were as follows [16]: In an argon atmosphere, 50 mg BP powder was dispersed in 50 mL anaerobic water. Follow on, the above solution was fully stirred and then sonicated for 8 h in the ice bath. Then the solution was centrifuged at 3000 rpm/min for 15 min to remove the unexfoliated BP powder. After that, the suspension was collected and go through multiple washing and centrifugations with ethanol and water. At last, the sample was dried by a freeze dryer.

N-CDs was prepared by the hydrothermal method from cellulose as raw material. Following a published method [17], FeCl2 (4 mg) and cellulose (2 g) were added to 30% wt H2O2 (100 mL) and sonicated for 5 min to completely disperse. This mixture solution was stirred for 6 h at 60 °C, washed three times, and freeze-dried to acquire the carboxylated cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs). The 500 mg of carboxylated CNCs was dispersed in deionized water and sonicated for 10 min, to well dispersed carboxylated CNCs aqueous solution. Follow on, the solution mixed with a proper amount of ammonia is transferred into a 100 mL stainless-steel autoclave, and placed in an oven 200 °C for 8 h [18]. After cooled to 25 °C, the brownish-black suspension was centrifuged at 8000 rpm/min for 30 min, and then the brown suspension was dialyzed with deionized water in dialysis membrane (MWCO = 1000 DA) for 3 days to get rid of unreacted reagents. Finally, freeze drying for two days, the purified N-CDs powders was obtained.

To investigate the optical property of N-CDs, various experiments were conducted. Briefly, the quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs was measured relative to Rhodamine B (RhB) [19]. A series of samples were taken under the same spectrometer conditions (RT, excitation wavelength of 275 nm) thrice and averaged. The UV absorbance values of N-CDs and RhB were kept under 0.1, to minimize the reabsorption effect [20]. The relative QY of N-CDs was calculated by the following Eq. (1):
(1)
Q
Y
s a m p l e
= Q
Y
r e f
×
I sample
I ref
×
O
D ref
O
D sample
×
n sample 2
n ref 2
where QYsample was the quantum yield of N-CDs, QYref represented the quantum yield of Rhodamine B (QY: 31%, in water). I sample and I ref were the measured integrated fluorescence emission intensity of N-CDs and Rhodamine B. The absorbance value of N-CDs and Rhodamine B denote the OD sample and OD ref, n sample and n ref were taken water as the medium, corresponding to the refractive index of 1.33 [21].

Moreover, the optical stability of N-CDs, as a crucial performance, was evaluated by fluorescence emission intensity. The N-CDs were preserved in the fridge at 4 °C for 5 days, and then the fluorescence intensity was recorded daily by fluorescence spectrophotometer.

EDC (5.7 mg) and NHS (3.5 mg) were dissolved in 1 mg/mL N-CDs solution and stirred for 12 h at 4 °C [22]. Followed the sediment was collected by three washing (8000 rpm/min, 45 min) to remove unreacted reagents. Next, Anti-Aβ (1 μg/mL) was added to the above solution incubated (4 h, 4 °C), and purified by washing with distilled water to obtain pure N-CDs@Anti-Aβ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Aβ by fluorescent switch
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CDs@Anti-Aβ (0.2 mg/mL) was dispersed to a 1.8 mL PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4), and incubated with various concentrations of BP nanosheets solution (10 mg/mL, pH 7.4) at RT for 5 min. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@Anti-Aβ reduced with the concentration of BP nanosheets. Thus, a fluorescence analysis method based on N-CDs@Anti-Aβ/BP nanosheets were established. For assay of Aβ, 10 μL of Aβ solution with different concentrations were injected into the above mixture. After incubation, the solution of fluorescence emission intensity was recovered. All samples were three times measured and the data represented the mean ± standard deviation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluating the selectivity of fluorescence analysis method
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-interference of fluorescence analysis method was assayed by parallelly several interferents (1 mg/mL, including ACSO, MGO, BSA, Lys, and PSA) mixed with Aβ (10 μg/mL) as a complex solution. Next, the mixture solution was added to the fluorescence detection platform (N-CDs@Anti-Aβ/BP nanosheets) in parallel, and the effect of various interferents on the fluorescence detection platform was recorded. All samples were measured by a Hitachi F-7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer three times.

The reusability of the fluorescence analysis method was evaluated by FT-IR. The Aβ (100 μL, 0.1 μg/mL) was added into N-CDs@Anti-Aβ/BP nanosheets (2 mL, RT). After incubation for 5 min at room temperature, the upper solution and precipitate were successfully collected from the solution by differential centrifugation. Finally, the separated solution and sediment were freeze dried and measured by FT-IR.

The ability of the fluorescence detection platform to reduce false-positive was evaluated by fluorescence spectrophotometry. In brief, the Aβ (50 μL, 0.1 μg/mL) was injected into a certain amount of BP nanosheets, N-CDs@Anti-Aβ, and N-CDs@Anti-Aβ/BP nanosheets, respectively. After incubation, three samples were recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometer. Further, the amount of Anti-Aβ, BP nanosheets, and Anti-Aβ combined with BP nanosheets were studied through FT-IR, for analysis of the bonding between Anti-Aβ and BP nanosheets.

The morphology of BP nanosheets were investigated by TEM. BP nanosheets exhibited a 2D structure (about 50–80 nm) with flatter surface (Fig. 1 A). The visible lattice spacing is 0.26 nm, which is assigned to the (040) plane of BP (Fig. 1B) [23]. AFM image indicated that the thickness of BP nanosheets is 1.29 nm. The monolayer phosphorene is around 0.53 nm, thickness shown in the spectra corresponds to 2 layer of single phosphorene layers (Fig. 1C) [24]. The structure of exfoliated BP nanosheets were studied by Raman spectroscopy. Three typical prominent vibration peaks of BP nanosheets were observed, classifying as one out-of-plane phonon mode Ag 1 (362.7 cm−1), and two in-plane modes B2g (439.5 cm−1) as well as Ag 2 (467.3 cm−1), respectively (Fig. 1D) [25]. The Raman peaks of BP nanosheets were slightly shifted to the high wave-number compared with bulk BP, indicating that the thickness of BP nanosheets decreased and the vibration frequency of P-atom between layers increased [26,27]. Full-scan XPS (Fig. 1E) of the BP nanosheets showed C 1s (284.7 eV), O 1s (532.0 eV), and P 2p (130.1 eV) peaks [28,29]. For P 2p spectra, PxOy sub-bands (132.6 eV) were observed (Fig. 1F), arising from surface oxygen defects or suboxides [30]. The peaks at 130.7 eV (P 2P1/2) and 129.8 eV (P 2P3/2) were corresponding to crystalline BP nanosheets (Fig. 1F) [31]. These results confirmed that BP nanosheets were successfully prepared.

The prepared N-CDs presented amorphous, well-dispersed and a uniform size of ∼1.9 nm (Fig. 2 A and inset in Figure S1). HR-TEM image showed the interplanar distance of 0.21 nm in N-CDs, corresponding to the (100 facet) plan lattice of graphite (inset Fig. 2A) [32]. The chemical composition of N-CDs was further analyzed using XPS. The result showed that the surface of N-CDs contained carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen (Fig. 2B). The C 1s core level spectra at 288.2 eV, 286.3 eV, and 284.8 eV are attributed to the CO [33], C–O [34], and C–C [35], respectively (Fig. 2C). The O 1s showed C–O [36], CO [37], and O–H [38] at peaks 535.4 eV, 532.7 eV, and 531.1 eV (Fig. 2D). The banding of N 1s XPS spectra (Figure S2) showed N–H (401.5 eV) and C–NC (399.5 eV) in N-CDs [39]. The TEM and XPS results indicate that the N-CDs have been successfully acquired. When Rhodamine B was used as the reference to measure QY, N-CDs was illustrating a very good QY of 14% (Figure S3). The high QY probably arises from nitrogen-doped in the process of prepare N-CDs [40]. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs fluctuated slight after place 5 days in PBS solution at 4 °C, showing good stability (Figure S4) [41]. This is related to the O–H, N–H in N-CDs [18]. Furthermore, Figure S5 showed the morphology of N-CDs and BP nanosheets compared to bare BP nanosheets (Fig. 1A), N-CDs/BP nanosheets exhibited a uniform distribution of N-CDs on the BP nanosheets, indicating that N-CDs were bound to the BP nanosheets and unaggregated.

Fluorescence analysis method is prone to false positive issue, limiting its application and development in the detection of diseases such as Alzheimer's disease [12]. To reduce this defect, a constructed “on-off-on” mode fluorescence analysis method was designed based on the BP nanosheets-regulated N-CDs modification Anti-Aβ (N-CDs@Anti-Aβ) for recognition Aβ in this work. To obtain the suitable Anti-Aβ concentration, saturation assays with various amounts of anti-Aβ mixed with 10 ng/mL Aβ was measured [42], and reached the maximum fluorescence response value about 2 μg/mL anti-Aβ (Figure S6). Since the large number of antibody molecules will form multilayer antibodies and affect the adsorption results [43,44], the subsaturated of anti-Aβ (1 μg/mL) was selected for further study.

The result showed that the maximal emission fluorescence intensity with a tendency to increase and then decrease with the redshift of the excitation wavelength from 300 to 400 nm in N-CDs@Anti-Aβ solution (Fig. 3 A). The maximum emission wavelength is 436 nm and excitation at 340 nm, thus, the 340 nm was selected as the optimum excitation wavelength for further experiments. The normalized emission spectra were developed. The emission peaks of N-CDs@Anti-Aβ were red-shifted with the increase of excitation wavelength (Fig. 3B). These results exhibited that the fluorescence emission spectroscopy of peak position and intensity were relying on the excitation wavelength.

The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@Anti-Aβ displayed negatively correlated with BP nanosheets content, and diminished with increase the concentrations of BP nanosheets ranging from 0 to 0.25 mg/mL (Fig. 3C). The quenching efficiency was up to 79.0% at BP nanosheets concentration of 0.25 mg/L (Fig. 3C and D). On the contrary, the fluorescence emission intensity of hybridization solution was gradually recovered with the increasing concentration of Aβ (0–20 ng/mL), when the above solution was successively added with Aβ and incubated (Fig. 3E). This result confirmed that BP nanosheets were a good switch. Furthermore, the analysis scopes for Aβ were from 0.25 to 2.50 ng/mL and from 2.5 to 15 ng/mL, respectively. The linear regression equation of Formula (2) and (3) were obtained:
(2) (F–F0)/F0 = 0.593 CAβ
(ng/mL) +0.0256
(3) (F–F0)/F0 = 0.0630 CAβ
(ng/mL) +1.421
where the presence or absence of Aβ in solution is expressed as F and F0, respectively.

The correlation coefficient value was 0.999 and 0.977 (Fig. 3F). The limit-of-detection (LOD) was 83 pg/mL (S/N = 3). This fluorescent switch-based fluorescence detection platform compared with other conventional fluorescence analysis method for detection of Aβ, revealing its superiority in the respect of broad linear range and higher detection limit (Table S1).

To further explore the mechanism of accurate recognition Aβ by BP nanosheets-regulated N-CDs@Anti-Aβ, the UV–vis, zeta potentials, and fluorescence lifetime decay were performed. Absorption spectrum of BP nanosheets overlapped well with emission and excitation spectra of N-CDs@Anti-Aβ (Fig. 4 A), indicating that the accurate identification of Aβ was related to the IFE [45]. The zeta potential value of N-CDs@Anti-Aβ and BP nanosheets were −13.57 mV and −6.17 mV, respectively (Fig. 4B), illustrating that BP nanosheets were not bonded to the N-CDs@Anti-Aβ position by electrostatic attraction [46]. The fluorescent lifetime of N-CDs@Anti-Aβ was slightly decreased along with addition of BP nanosheets (Fig. 4C and D), demonstrating a static quenching between BP nanosheets and Anti-Aβ, which is further confirmed the IFE mechanism [47]. Hence, the achievement of accurate identification of Aβ by BP nanosheets controlling N-CDs@Anti-Aβ was attributed to the IFE between N-CDs@Anti-Aβ and BP nanosheets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent switch reduce false positives and regeneration
ce:section.ce:para: The reduced false positive and reusability of analysis methods were key factors for practical application [48,49]. In this work, the performance of our fluorescence detection in the false-positive and recoverability of BP nanosheets was further researched by fluorospectrophotometer and FT-IR spectrometer. The Aβ was introduced into N-CDs@Anti-Aβ, BP nanosheets and N-CDs@Anti-Aβ/BP nanosheets, respectively. In the absence of BP nanosheets, the fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs@Anti-Aβ and N-CQDs@Anti-Aβ/Aβ were overlapped perfectly (Fig. 5 A), indicating alone N-CDs@Anti-Aβ failure to recognize Aβ. Similarly, the fluorescence spectra between BP nanosheets and BP nanosheets/Aβ were unchanged indicating that BP nanosheets also unidentified Aβ. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity was only recovered when both N-CQDs@Anti-Aβ and BP nanosheets were present, after the introduction of Aβ into the solution. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of the solution was linearly fitted to the concentration of Aβ. Further, the BP nanosheets@Anti-Aβ (Fig. 5B) appears two peaks at ∼1156 cm−1 and ∼1073 cm−1 which were derived from the PO and P–O bands in anti-Aβ [50]. Results indicated that the anti-Aβ was conjugated with BP nanosheets contributed to enhancing the stability of anti-Aβ. Besides, some interference in the serum will nonspecifically bind with BP nanosheets or N-CDs, leading to low sensitive and false positive result [51]. To examine the influence of serum on our fluorescence detection platform, a 50 μL of diluted serum and deionized water was incubated with N-CDs for 10 min, respectively. The emission spectra of N-CDs slightly reduced with the introduce diluted serum and overlap with the addition of DW (Figure S8A). Similarly, the BP nanosheets addition of diluted serum and DW were also mostly overlap (Figure S8B). These results indicated that the fluorescence of BP nanosheets and N-CDs were unaffected by diluted serum [52,53]. Thus, the problem of false positive in recognition process was reduced by fluorescence analysis method controlled by BP nanosheet.

To further validate the BP nanosheets as fluorescent switch for regenerate, the BP nanosheets were department by differential centrifugation. The N-CDs, N-CDs@Anti-Aβ, and the separated solution were characterized by FT-IR (Figure S9). The N-CDs surface has been characteristic absorption bands of N–H stretching vibrations at 3475-3350 cm−1 due to nitrogen-doping [54]. The new absorption peaks of C–N stretch (1353 cm−1) [55] and C–O bend (1130,1077 cm−1) [56] were observed in the N-CDs@Anti-Aβ (Figure S9), confirming the successful linkage of anti-Aβ to the N-CDs. The major peaks of the separated solution are coincided with N-CDs@Anti-Aβ (Figure S9). Further, the three typical peaks of BP nanosheets and precipitation are consistent with the results which both contain PO (1664 cm−1) [57] and C–P (1451 cm−1,852 cm−1) [58] (Figure S9). These results indicate that BP nanosheets have been successfully regenerated.

The anti-interference capacity was studied for the Aβ by antibody, protein and amino acid as interference substances. The result showed that the concentrations of interferent were much higher than Aβ. Further, the fluorescence of mixed solution did not change significantly, indicating this fluorescence analysis method with good anti-interference for the detection of Aβ (Figure S7). The actual samples were examined to appraise the feasibility of fluorescence analysis method. Artifical serum samples were diluted with PBS (pH = 7.4, 0.1 M) with drop in known concentrations of the N-CDs@Anti-Aβ, BP nanosheets, and Aβ in turn for reaction. As listed in Table S2, the recoveries of the fluorescence detection platform were in the range of 95.8%–122.4% and RSD ranged from 0.28% to 4.1%, suggesting that the designed fluorescence analysis method possesses potential application for detecting Aβ in future.

In summary, an “on−off−on” method indirect fluorescence sensing platform has been successfully constructed for the exact and sensitive determination of Aβ. The Aβ was rapidly recognized using the BP controlling N-CDs@Anti-Aβ fluorescence, and reduced false positive results arising from unstable Anti-Aβ. Moreover, the BP nanosheets were successfully regenerated via differential centrifugation approach. Thus, this analysis method indicated a significant advantage in Aβ detection with an excellent sensitivity of 83 pg/mL and linearity (the range from 0.25 to 15.0 ng/mL), as well as show well anti-interference. Good precision and recoveries from 95.8% to 122.4% were also observed in a real sample. This work proposed a regenerable and reducing false-positive fluorescent switch enlightening for design and application of new sensing platforms.

Shuang Tan: Investigation; Data curation; Writing-review draft & editing. Shunling Li: Conceptualization; Methodology; Formal analysis; Writing-review & editing. Congkui Tang: Resources; Formal analysis. Xiongfei Bai: Formal analysis; Investigation. Xin Ran: Data curation; Investigation. Qing Qu: Project administration; Funding acquisition; Formal analysis; Writing-review & editing. Lei Li: Supervision; Project administration. Long Yang: Resources; Funding acquisition; Writing-review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under the Grants 52061041, 51661033, 31660538, 21864024, 51361028, 51161025, 32171884, and 32060323. This work was also supported by the Applied Basic Research Program of Yunnan Province (No. 202001AW070017, 202101AT070047) and Agriculture Joint Research Program of Yunnan Province (No. 2018FG001-006). Authors thank Analysis and Measurement Center of Yunnan University for the sample testing service.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2022.123461.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Alzheimer's disease-related dysregulation of mRNA translation causes key pathological features with ageing
Transl. Psychiatry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Ghosh, K. Mizuno, S.S. Tiwari, P. Proitsi, B. Gomez Perez-Nievas, E. Glennon, R.T. Martinez-Nunez, K.P. Giese, Alzheimer's disease-related dysregulation of mRNA translation causes key pathological features with ageing, Transl Psychiatry 10(1) (2020) 192.
